topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The ballroom glittered under a thousand suspended lights, a dazzling spectacle that did little to soothe the knot tightening in my stomach. I clutched my glass of champagne, the crystal cool against my palm, and scanned the room, a frantic search for a familiar silhouette. Then, there she was. Across the dance floor, laughing with a group of people I vaguely recognized, stood Alex. My breath hitched, a shallow, sharp intake of air. Every muscle in my body seemed to tense, preparing for an impact I desperately wanted to avoid. I forced a smile, a brittle thing that felt glued to my face, and began a slow, deliberate pivot towards the refreshment table, needing distance, needing an escape route.

[story 2]

"Oh, wow, look who it is!" Sarah’s voice boomed, pulling me out of my internal spiral. I managed a weak nod, my eyes darting towards the entrance. And there he was. Standing near the floral arrangements, looking impossibly composed in a tailored suit, was Mark. A cold wave washed over me, making my hands clammy. I felt a sudden urge to melt into the wallpaper. I took a long, unnecessary sip of water, my gaze fixed on the intricate pattern of the carpet, trying to anchor myself to something solid as my pulse hammered against my ribs. I needed to find a quiet corner, anywhere, before I had to face him.

[story 3]

I was halfway through a conversation with a mutual acquaintance when the air in the room seemed to thin. My peripheral vision caught movement, and my entire frame froze mid-gesture. It was him. Liam. He was leaning against a pillar, and the way he turned his head, catching my eye across the crowded room, sent a jolt straight through me. My mouth went dry. I suddenly felt an intense pressure behind my eyes, a desperate need to simply disappear. I quickly turned my attention to the speaker on stage, my movements stiff and exaggeratedly casual. I focused on the music, letting the rhythm wash over me, praying the moment would pass before I had to acknowledge the reality of his presence.
